title: Testing the Spirits and Love Perfected
subtitle: Discerning true and false spirits, God is love, love casts out fear

heb.paragraph: Test the spirits to see whether they are from God - discernment is required because many false prophets have gone out.
Every spirit that confesses Jesus Christ has come in the flesh is from God - the christological test is incarnation.
You have overcome them, for he who is in you is greater - victory is assured because the indwelling God exceeds all opponents.

refs.note: No one speaking in the Spirit of God says Jesus is accursed.
Those who do not confess Jesus Christ coming in the flesh - this is the deceiver.

notes.note: Test the spirits - many false prophets have gone out. The Spirit of God confesses Jesus Christ has come in flesh. The spirit of antichrist denies this.
You have overcome them - he who is in you is greater. They are from the world; we are from God. By this we know truth and error.
Calvin emphasizes the christological criterion: true faith confesses incarnation. Denial of Jesus humanity is the antichrist spirit.
Victory is not by our strength but by indwelling God. The greater one within guarantees triumph over external opponents.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: Come in the flesh (en sarki elelythota) - perfect participle emphasizes the ongoing reality of incarnation. Jesus remains human. The NET Bible's textual notes draw attention to variant readings and translation challenges in this passage. The translators weigh manuscript evidence alongside syntactic analysis to arrive at renderings that balance accuracy with readability, providing supplementary notes where the original resists simple translation.

Spirit of truth and spirit of error - the contrast anticipates the explicit mention of the Spirit of truth in John 14-16. The NET Bible's textual notes draw attention to variant readings and translation challenges in this passage. The translators weigh manuscript evidence alongside syntactic analysis to arrive at renderings that balance accuracy with readability, providing supplementary notes where the original resists simple translation.

yarbrough.source: Robert Yarbrough, BECNT 1-3 John — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Yarbrough emphasizes that testing spirits is not optional but commanded. Discernment is a spiritual discipline. Yarbrough traces the epistle's logic through its characteristic contrasts: light and darkness, truth and falsehood, love and hate. The author's binary rhetoric is not simplistic but diagnostic — it exposes the fundamental orientation of the heart and calls readers to genuine self-examination in the light of God's revealed character.

kruse.source: Colin Kruse, TNTC/Pillar 1-3 John — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Kruse notes that the false teachers likely denied Jesus humanity (docetism). The incarnation is non-negotiable. Kruse's analysis focuses on the letter's pastoral function within its historical context. The author addresses concrete community concerns — false teaching, relational fracture, assurance of salvation — with theological precision that grounds pastoral care in the foundational realities of the incarnation and atonement.

hist.historical: First John addresses a crisis caused by secessionists who have left the Johannine community (2:19). These opponents likely held proto-Gnostic views: denying that Jesus Christ came 'in the flesh' (4:2), claiming sinless perfection while living immorally (1:8-10), and failing to love fellow believers (2:9-11). The author, traditionally identified as the apostle John writing from Ephesus in the 90s AD, establishes tests of authentic Christianity: right belief about Christ's incarnation, righteous living, and love for the community. The letter's repetitive, spiral structure reinforces these themes through variation rather than linear argument. The community had experienced a traumatic split, and the letter reassures those who remained of their standing with God.
hist.context: Beloved, do not believe every spirit, but test the spirits to see whether they are from God, for many false prophets have gone out into the world. By this you know the Spirit of God: every spirit that confesses that Jesus Christ has come in the flesh is from God, and every spirit that does not confess Jesus is not from God. This is the spirit of the antichrist, which you heard was coming and now is in the world already. Little children, you are from God and have overcome them, for he who is in you is greater than he who is in the world. They are from the world; therefore they speak from the world, and the world listens to them. We are from God. Whoever knows God listens to us; whoever is not from God does not listen to us. By this we know the Spirit of truth and the spirit of error.

heb.paragraph: God is love - not merely that God loves but that love defines his essential nature. Love is who God is.

heb.gloss: only, unique, one of a kind
heb.paragraph: God sent his only Son into the world - the unique Son was given, not created. The gift demonstrates love.
No one has ever seen God - yet if we love one another, God abides in us. Love makes the invisible God visible.

refs.note: God so loved the world that he gave his only Son.
God shows his love for us in that while we were sinners, Christ died for us.
No one has ever seen God; the only God who is at the Father side has made him known.
By this all people will know you are my disciples, if you have love for one another.

notes.note: Love one another, for love is from God. Whoever loves is born of God and knows God. Whoever does not love does not know God, because God is love.
God love was made manifest: he sent his only Son that we might live. This is love - not that we loved God but that he loved us and sent his Son as propitiation.
If God so loved us, we ought to love one another. No one has seen God; if we love, God abides in us and his love is perfected in us.

Calvin notes that God is love is not reversible - love is not God. Love defines God; God is the source of all true love. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: God is love (ho theos agape estin) - one of the most profound theological statements in Scripture. Love is not one attribute among many but the essence.
Love is perfected (teteleiomene) - brought to completion, reaches its goal. Divine love achieves its purpose when we love.

kruse.source: Colin Kruse, TNTC/Pillar 1-3 John — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Kruse notes that the invisible God becomes visible through Christian love. The church displays God character to the world. Kruse's analysis focuses on the letter's pastoral function within its historical context. The author addresses concrete community concerns — false teaching, relational fracture, assurance of salvation — with theological precision that grounds pastoral care in the foundational realities of the incarnation and atonement.

hist.context: Beloved, let us love one another, for love is from God, and whoever loves has been born of God and knows God. Whoever does not love does not know God, because God is love. In this the love of God was made manifest among us, that God sent his only Son into the world so that we might live through him. In this is love, not that we have loved God but that he loved us and sent his Son to be the propitiation for our sins. Beloved, if God so loved us, we also ought to love one another. No one has ever seen God; if we love one another, God abides in us and his love is perfected in us.

heb.gloss: to perfect, complete, bring to maturity
heb.paragraph: By this is love perfected with us - love reaches its intended goal. Perfected love produces confidence.
There is no fear in love, but perfect love casts out fear - fear and mature love are incompatible. Fear involves punishment.
If anyone says I love God and hates his brother, he is a liar - love for visible brother tests love for invisible God.

refs.note: You did not receive the spirit of slavery to fall back into fear.
Deliver all those who through fear of death were subject to lifelong slavery.
We have heard for ourselves, and we know that this is indeed the Savior of the world.
You shall love the LORD your God with all your heart.

notes.note: We abide in him because he has given us his Spirit. The Father sent his Son to be Savior. Whoever confesses Jesus is the Son, God abides in him. God is love.
Love is perfected so we may have confidence for judgment day. Perfect love casts out fear. Fear has to do with punishment.
We love because he first loved us. Claiming to love God while hating brother is lying. The commandment: whoever loves God must love his brother.
Calvin notes that fear of judgment reveals incomplete love. As love matures, fear recedes. Perfect love produces bold assurance.
The logic is irrefutable: if you cannot love the visible brother, you cannot love the invisible God. Love for God is tested by love for people.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: As he is so also are we in this world - believers share Christ nature and mission now, not just in the future. The NET Bible's textual notes draw attention to variant readings and translation challenges in this passage. The translators weigh manuscript evidence alongside syntactic analysis to arrive at renderings that balance accuracy with readability, providing supplementary notes where the original resists simple translation.

Fear has to do with punishment (kolasin echei) - the fear in view is terror of condemnation, not healthy reverence. The NET Bible's textual notes draw attention to variant readings and translation challenges in this passage. The translators weigh manuscript evidence alongside syntactic analysis to arrive at renderings that balance accuracy with readability, providing supplementary notes where the original resists simple translation.

yarbrough.source: Robert Yarbrough, BECNT 1-3 John — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Yarbrough emphasizes the practical test: love for brother is visible evidence of love for God. Vertical and horizontal are inseparable. Yarbrough traces the epistle's logic through its characteristic contrasts: light and darkness, truth and falsehood, love and hate. The author's binary rhetoric is not simplistic but diagnostic — it exposes the fundamental orientation of the heart and calls readers to genuine self-examination in the light of God's revealed character.

hist.context: By this we know that we abide in him and he in us, because he has given us of his Spirit. And we have seen and testify that the Father has sent his Son to be the Savior of the world. Whoever confesses that Jesus is the Son of God, God abides in him and he in God. So we have come to know and to believe the love that God has for us. God is love, and whoever abides in love abides in God, and God abides in him. By this is love perfected with us, so that we may have confidence for the day of judgment, because as he is so also are we in this world. There is no fear in love, but perfect love casts out fear. For fear has to do with punishment, and whoever fears has not been perfected in love. We love because he first loved us. If anyone says I love God and hates his brother, he is a liar; for he who does not love his brother whom he has seen cannot love God whom he has not seen. And this commandment we have from him: whoever loves God must also love his brother.

themes.note: Mercy dominates through the God is love declaration. Faith is tested by christological confession. Mission appears in the Savior of the world title.

rows.label: vv. 7-12
rows.text: God is love; sent his Son

rows.label: vv. 13-21
rows.text: Perfect love casts out fear; love brother

lit.note: Chapter 4 combines christological confession with brotherly love. God is love becomes the ground of ethics and the source of confidence.

discourse.title: 1 John 4 — Testing Spirits and God's Love Perfected
discourse.thesis: Test spirits by their confession of Christ incarnate. God is love; his love is perfected in us so that we love one another.

nodes.label: Test the spirits (4:1-6)
nodes.text: Do not believe every spirit, but test them. Every spirit that confesses Jesus Christ come in the flesh is from God; every spirit that does not is the spirit of the antichrist. You are from God and have overcome them, because he who is in you is greater than he who is in the world.

nodes.label: God is love (4:7-12)
nodes.text: Let us love one another, for love is from God. Whoever loves has been born of God and knows God. Whoever does not love does not know God, for God is love. God sent his only Son into the world so that we might live through him — this is love, not that we loved God but that he loved us and sent his Son as propitiation. If God so loved us, we ought to love one another. No one has ever seen God; if we love one another, God abides in us and his love is perfected in us.

nodes.label: Abiding in love (4:13-16)
nodes.text: By this we know we abide in him and he in us: he has given us of his Spirit. We have seen and testify that the Father has sent his Son as Savior. Whoever confesses that Jesus is the Son of God, God abides in him. We have come to know and believe the love God has for us. God is love, and whoever abides in love abides in God.

nodes.label: Perfect love casts out fear (4:17-21)
nodes.text: By this love is perfected: we have confidence for the day of judgment because as he is so are we in this world. There is no fear in love; perfect love casts out fear. We love because he first loved us. Whoever claims to love God but hates his brother is a liar. He who loves God must also love his brother.

discourse.note: The double declaration 'God is love' (4:8, 16) is the theological summit of the letter.

hebtext.note: v.1's discernment imperative. Christians are not to receive every claim-of-Spirit-inspiration uncritically but to test (dokimazō — assay-tested for genuineness, like metals tested with fire). The chapter provides specific Christological-test (vv.2-3) for distinguishing genuine from counterfeit spiritual claims.

v.7's foundational love-source theology. Christian-love is not autonomous-human-virtue but participation-in-divine-love. The chapter develops this radically: 'God is love' (v.8, 16) — God's character itself is love. The verses anchor Christian theology of love as theologically-grounded rather than merely ethical-imperative.

hebtext.word: φόβος οὐκ ἔστιν ἐν τῇ ἀγάπῃ
hebtext.tlit: phobos ouk estin en tē agapē
hebtext.note: v.18's beautiful formulation: 'perfect love casts out fear.' The fear in view is judgment-fear; mature-believer-love expels this dread. The verse does not contradict 'fear of the LORD' (which is reverent-awe, different category) but frees believers from punitive-judgment-fear before God who is love.

ppl.name: False prophets / false spirits
ppl.text: vv.1-6 address community-wide concern about competing-spiritual-claims. Multiple groups claimed Spirit-inspiration; John provides Christological-test for genuineness. The discernment-criterion is incarnational-Christology: confessing Jesus-Christ-come-in-the-flesh marks genuine Spirit; denial marks the spirit of antichrist.

ppl.name: God as love
ppl.text: 1 John 4:8 and 4:16's twin declarations 'God is love' are among NT's most-cited theological statements. The verse identifies love as God's character — not merely his action but his essence. Christian theology of God's-attributes works within these verses' framework.

ppl.name: Christ as propitiation / atoning-sacrifice
ppl.text: vv.9-10's 'God showed his love among us by sending his only Son... and sent him as the atoning sacrifice (hilasmos)' grounds love-of-God in concrete-Christological action. Christian-love-theology is not abstract — it is centered on Christ's-death-as-divine-self-giving.

ppl.name: Believers as loving-community
ppl.role: Visible demonstration of God's invisible love (v.12)
ppl.text: v.12's 'no one has ever seen God; if we love one another, God lives in us' establishes ecclesial-community as God's invisible-love-made-visible. The chapter's pastoral-burden is concrete: love-among-believers is not optional-decoration but theological-necessity for genuine community.

rec.text: Augustine's homiletical exposition of 'God is love' became one of Christian theology's foundational texts on God's character. His pairing of love-of-God with love-of-neighbor ('whoever does not love his brother whom he has seen cannot love God whom he has not seen,' v.20) shaped Western-Christian theology of caritas as the supreme-virtue.

rec.who: Bernard of Clairvaux, On the Love of God (c. 1130 CE)
rec.text: Bernard's classic monastic-spiritual-treatise drew extensively on 1 John 4's God-is-love framework. His four-degrees-of-love structure (loving-self-for-self, loving-God-for-self, loving-God-for-God, loving-self-for-God) develops the chapter's love-theology into spiritual-formation framework.

Barth's theological-anthropology drew foundationally on 'God is love.' His insistence that God's-being-and-acting are inseparable shapes 20th-century-theology of God's-character as fundamentally love-orientated. The verse anchors Barth's broader Trinitarian theology.

Brown's exegetical reading of 4:1-6's spirit-discernment passage emphasises its community-historical context: the Johannine-community had recently experienced the secession of those denying Christ's-incarnation. The discernment-criterion is not abstract-theological-test but concrete-community-experience-criterion.

thread.text: Spirit-discernment imperative parallels Pauline 'distinguishing of spirits' (1 Cor 12:10) and 'test all things' (1 Thess 5:21). Multiple NT writers converge on the necessity of believer-discernment of Spirit-claims. The principle of testing-not-uncritically-receiving is consistent NT teaching.

Christological-test for spirits (Jesus Christ come in the flesh) parallels 2 John 7 and Pauline 'no one can say Jesus is Lord except by the Spirit' (1 Cor 12:3). Multiple NT writers anchor Spirit-discernment in confession of Christ's lordship and incarnation.

'God is love' develops the OT's 'compassionate and gracious God, slow to anger, abounding in love' (Exod 34:6; Ps 86:15). 1 John's compressed formulation distills the OT's extensive divine-love-language into ontological-character statement. The OT-NT continuity is theologically programmatic.

'Perfect love casts out fear' parallels Rom 8:15's 'spirit of adoption, not of slavery to fall back into fear' and 2 Tim 1:7's 'spirit not of fear but of power and love.' Multiple NT writers connect mature-Christian-experience with displacement-of-fear by Spirit-empowered love.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVDear friends, do not believe every spirit, but test the spirits to see whether they are from God, because many false prophets have gone out into the world.KJVBeloved, believe not every spirit, but try the spirits whether they are of God: because many false prophets are gone out into the world.NIVDear friends, let us love one another, for love comes from God. Everyone who loves has been born of God and knows God.KJVBeloved, let us love one another: for love is of God; and every one that loveth is born of God, and knoweth God.NIVWhoever does not love does not know God, because God is love.KJVHe that loveth not knoweth not God; for God is love.NIVThis is love: not that we loved God, but that he loved us and sent his Son as an atoning sacrifice for our sins.KJVHerein is love, not that we loved God, but that he loved us, and sent his Son to be the propitiation for our sins.NIVNo one has ever seen God; but if we love one another, God lives in us and his love is made complete in us.KJVNo man hath seen God at any time. If we love one another, God dwelleth in us, and his love is perfected in us.NIVAnd so we know and rely on the love God has for us. God is love. Whoever lives in love lives in God, and God in them.KJVAnd we have known and believed the love that God hath to us. God is love; and he that dwelleth in love dwelleth in God, and God in him.NIVThere is no fear in love. But perfect love drives out fear, because fear has to do with punishment. The one who fears is not made perfect in love.KJVThere is no fear in love; but perfect love casteth out fear: because fear hath torment. He that feareth is not made perfect in love.NIVWe love because he first loved us.KJVWe love him, because he first loved us.

coaching.tip: 'Test the spirits to see whether they are from God' (v. 1). Not all spiritual experience is divine. The test: confession that 'Jesus Christ has come in the flesh' (v. 2). The heretics denied incarnation — too spiritual for real flesh. John insists: denying the body denies the Lord. Christology filters prophecy.

'Perfect love casts out fear' (v. 18). Fear and love don't coexist at maturity. Fear 'has to do with punishment' — but those who love know they're loved. Confidence replaces anxiety. This isn't denial of God's holiness but assurance of God's embrace. The beloved need not fear the Lover.

prayer_prompt: As John declares that perfect love drives out fear, consider asking God which fear in your life is evidence that you have not yet fully received the depth of His love for you.
